title: Event Loop Order
description:
Predict the output order, then run to verify.

The event loop processes the call stack first, then drains all microtasks (Promises), then picks the next macrotask (setTimeout/setInterval).

Change the code to test your understanding — swap things around and see what happens.
hints: Synchronous code always runs first, top to bottom.
Promise .then callbacks are microtasks — they run before any macrotask.
setTimeout is a macrotask — it runs after the microtask queue is empty.

title: Fix the Closure Bug
description:
This loop prints '5' five times instead of 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.

Fix it two different ways:
1. Replace `var` with `let`
2. Use an IIFE to create a new scope per iteration

Explain why `var` causes the bug.
hints: var is function-scoped, not block-scoped — all iterations share the same `i`.
By the time the callbacks run, the loop has finished and `i` is 5.
let creates a new binding per iteration — each closure captures its own `i`.

title: Implement once()
description:
Implement a `once(fn)` higher-order function that returns a new function.
The returned function calls `fn` on the first invocation and returns its result.
All subsequent calls return the same result without calling `fn` again.
hints: You need to track whether the function has been called yet.
Store the result of the first call in a variable in the closure.
Use a boolean flag `called` and cache the `result`.

title: Implement memoize()
description:
Implement `memoize(fn)` — a HOF that caches the result of `fn` based on its arguments.
If called again with the same arguments, return the cached result without re-running `fn`.
hints: Use a Map or object as the cache.
The cache key should be based on the arguments — JSON.stringify works for simple args.
Check if the key exists in cache before calling fn.

title: Implement debounce()
description:
Implement `debounce(fn, delay)` — returns a function that delays invoking `fn` until after `delay` ms have passed since the last call.

Used to avoid calling expensive functions (API calls, resize handlers) on every event.
hints: Use setTimeout to delay the call.
If called again before the delay expires, cancel the previous timer with clearTimeout.
Store the timer ID in a variable in the closure.

title: Implement flatten()
description:
Implement `flatten(arr)` that recursively flattens a nested array to any depth.
Do not use `Array.prototype.flat()`.
hints: Use Array.isArray() to detect nested arrays.
Recursion: if an element is an array, flatten it and spread the result.
reduce() + concat works well here, or use a simple for loop with push.

title: Implement curry()
description:
Implement `curry(fn)` that converts a multi-argument function into a curried version.
The curried function should accept arguments one at a time (or in groups) and only call `fn` when enough arguments have been collected.
hints: Check fn.length to know how many arguments are needed.
If enough args have been collected, call the original fn.
Otherwise, return a new function that collects more args (recursive currying).
Use rest params (...args) and spread to accumulate arguments.

title: Implement a Simple EventEmitter
description:
Implement an EventEmitter class with:
- `on(event, listener)` — register a listener
- `off(event, listener)` — remove a listener
- `emit(event, ...args)` — call all listeners for the event
- `once(event, listener)` — listener fires only once, then auto-removes
hints: Store listeners in a Map: event name → Set of listeners.
For once(), wrap the listener in a wrapper that calls off() after firing.
off() should remove only the specific listener function reference.

title: Implement Promise.all()
description:
Implement your own version of `Promise.all(promises)` without using the built-in.
It should resolve with an array of results when all promises resolve, and reject immediately if any promise rejects.
hints: Return a new Promise.
Track how many promises have resolved using a counter.
Store results in an array at the correct index (not push — order matters).
Reject immediately on the first rejection.

title: Implement a Pipeline (pipe)
description:
Implement `pipe(...fns)` that composes functions left-to-right.
The output of each function becomes the input to the next.
Also implement `pipeAsync(...fns)` that works with async functions.
hints: pipe() can be implemented with reduce: fns.reduce((acc, fn) => fn(acc), initialValue).
The reduce starts with the first argument passed to the piped function.
For pipeAsync, use reduce with Promise chaining: (acc, fn) => acc.then(fn).
